author: @m3sca1
text:
Intriguing how the voltage rise is gradual and not sudden.
My guess is you may have higher voltage and amps by replacing the Aluminium with Zinc.
A friend of mine showed me an old Zinc Air battery he found somewhere.
Having never seen or heard of a Zinc Air battery before I researched it.
Found out there is more than enough Zinc on the planet to run all the cars currently on the road ( if they were electric ), and the even better part was that Zinc Air lends itself to operating at higher current level if Air is pumped through it.
Behaves like a fuel cell.
This technology your tinkering with might behave the same with more oxygen....thanks to posting!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it is an interesting technology mate - there is plenty of magnesium and of course iron - all metal air batteries work in the same way - pumping it through makes it more of a redox flow - but still good stuff

replies.author: @steve42lawson
replies.text: Could that be due to capacitance?  The only "load" on the thing is the volt meter, which, most likely, has a very high impedance.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
Steve Lawson
i suppose so but i doubt it

author: @Slider2732
text:
Very cool, great to hear it's an active research area.
Green leaves are a bit of a prized commodity in November lol, but have wondered about this for a while....also, the use of red and blue LED's rather than white light for test readings.
I'd better get out to the garden !

author: @Pelukeitor
text:
Robert parace que los dos vamos por la misma direccion de las hojas como supercondensadores , lo unico malo que tiene este metodo es que se degrada al paso de unos dias. :-/

Robert seems it the two are on the same direction of the leaf as supercapacitors, bad only having this method is that it degrades in a few days. : - /


Vegetal electrolyte     Publicado el 22/7/2014

Supercondensador con hoja y grafeno 3         Publicado el 8/3/2014
